question: What would training for a young American tank commander look like?
short_answer: It would be a combined-arms, gunnery, radio, map-reading, and crew-coordination syllabus that teaches the commander to observe, report, and control the platoon, not just drive or shoot.
evidence_summary: FM 17-30 and FM 17-33 define the platoon and battalion role set.
FM 17-12 emphasizes gunnery drills; Devers shows the broader combined-arms framing.
Radio manuals and 16-line message format show that communication procedure is part of training, not an afterthought.

simulation_variables.effect: raises order quality and report quality.
improves the odds that reports and orders reach the right place.
reduces false contact pictures and stale map states.
improves with doctrine and command position, then narrows when buttoned.
rises when training produces consistent calls under pressure.

game_implications: The player should begin with a thin local picture and improve by learning procedures, not by unlocking omniscience.
Quick, disciplined radio and recon choices should matter more than raw vehicle strength in the opening slice.
